title: 1: The Playground
text:
Group field theory is a beautiful mathematical idea: that the whole of the universe emerges from the interplay of symmetries. We will begin with the simplest possible foundation: defining a set of symmetries, and then building a universe out of them. This is the playground, where we set up a series of simple rules, and then see what happens when we let them run.
The ‘group’ in ‘group field theory’ refers to a mathematical structure that captures the idea of symmetry. A group is a set of elements, along with an operation that combines two elements to produce a third. These elements represent symmetries, and the operation represents how these symmetries combine. For example, the symmetries of a square can be represented by a group consisting of rotations (by 90, 180, and 270 degrees), reflections in both the horizontal and vertical axes and through the corners, and the identity transformation (doing nothing). These symmetries can be combined in various ways: for example, a 90 degree rotation followed by a reflection across the vertical axis is equivalent to a reflection across the diagonal. The group captures all of these relationships between symmetries.
The symmetries of a square also include an important concept in group field theory: they are not commutative. This means that order in which you carry out the symmetry operations is important. For example, if you first rotate the square by 90 degrees and then reflect it across the vertical axis, you will end up in a different position than if you first reflect it and then rotate it. This non-commutativity is a key feature of the symmetries that we will be working with in group field theory, and it leads to rich and complex behavior when we start to combine these symmetries in various ways.



The symmetries of a square

title: 1: The Playground
text:
Group field theory is a beautiful mathematical idea: that the whole of the universe emerges from the interplay of symmetries. We will begin with the simplest possible foundation: defining a set of symmetries, and then building a universe out of them. This is the playground, where we set up a series of simple rules, and then see what happens when we let them run.
The ‘group’ in ‘group field theory’ refers to a mathematical structure that captures the idea of symmetry. A group is a set of elements, along with an operation that combines two elements to produce a third. These elements represent symmetries, and the operation represents how these symmetries combine. For example, the symmetries of a square can be represented by a group consisting of rotations (by 90, 180, and 270 degrees), reflections in both the horizontal and vertical axes and through the corners, and the identity transformation (doing nothing). These symmetries can be combined in various ways: for example, a 90 degree rotation followed by a reflection across the vertical axis is equivalent to a reflection across the diagonal. The group captures all of these relationships between symmetries.
The symmetries of a square also include an important concept in group field theory: they are not commutative. This means that order in which you carry out the symmetry operations is important. For example, if you first rotate the square by 90 degrees and then reflect it across the vertical axis, you will end up in a different position than if you first reflect it and then rotate it. This non-commutativity is a key feature of the symmetries that we will be working with in group field theory, and it leads to rich and complex behavior when we start to combine these symmetries in various ways.



The symmetries of a square

title: 1: The Playground
text:
2 The group
Let us introduce the group that we will be using to build our universe. It is a relatively complicated group, so once we have introduced it we will break it down and explain what each component means. The group is

G=\mathrm{SO}(4,1)\times \mathrm{U}(1) \times \mathrm{SU}(2) \times \mathrm{SU}(3).
\tag{1}
Let’s break it down. The first part, \mathrm{SO}(4,1), is the group of symmetries of a four-dimensional spacetime with one time dimension and three spatial dimensions. The second part, \mathrm{U}(1), is the group of symmetries associated with electromagnetism. The third part, \mathrm{SU}(2), is the group of symmetries associated with the weak nuclear force. The fourth part, \mathrm{SU}(3), is the group of symmetries associated with the strong nuclear force. We have chosen this group based on observations of our universe: it was a specific choice that we made in order to build a universe that resembles our own. However, we could have chosen a different group, and we would have ended up with a different universe. This is the playground: we can choose any group we like, and then see what kind of universe emerges from it.
Equation 1 is a special type of group called a semisimple Lie group. This gives it some very nice mathematical properties that we will be using to create our universe. In particular, Lie groups have a corresponding Lie algebra, which is a mathematical structure that captures the infinitesimal symmetries of the group. The Lie algebra allows us to work with the symmetries in a more manageable way, and it will be a key tool in our construction of the universe. Each element of a Lie group G can be expressed as the exponential of an element of its Lie algebra \mathfrak{g}, which is a very powerful property to help us analyse the group. As the group Equation 1 is a direct product of four underlying groups, the algebra is a direct sum of the underlying algebras (compare to how normal exponentials work: e^{a+b}=e^a e^b),

\mathfrak{g}=\mathfrak{so}(4,1)\oplus \mathfrak{u}(1) \oplus \mathfrak{su}(2) \oplus \mathfrak{su}(3).
\tag{2}
Each of these algebras is formed of a set of ‘generators’, which are the building blocks of the symmetries. The generators of \mathfrak{so}(4,1) are denoted by M_{AB}, where A and B run from 0 to 4. The generators combine together through a set of rules called the commutation relations, which capture how much the symmetries fail to commute. A commutator is defined simply as [X, Y] = XY - YX. The commutation relations for \mathfrak{so}(4,1) are given by

\left[M_{AB}, M_{CD}\right] = \left(\eta_{AC} M_{BD} - \eta_{AD} M_{BC} - \eta_{BC} M_{AD} + \eta_{BD} M_{AC}\right),
\tag{3}
where \eta_{AB}=\mathrm{diag}(-1,1,1,1,1). This looks a lot like the metric of special relativity, just with an extra spatial dimension: this will turn out not to be a coincidence!
\mathfrak{u}(1) only has a single generator, which we will denote by Q. The commutation relations for \mathfrak{u}(1) are trivial: [Q, Q] = 0 (because there is only one generator, and a generator always commutes with itself). An algebra where all generators commute is called ‘abelian’.
\mathfrak{su}(2) has three generators, which we will denote by J_i where i runs from 1 to 3. The commutation relations for \mathfrak{su}(2) are given by

\left[J_i, J_j\right] = i \epsilon_{ijk} J_k
\tag{4}
where \epsilon_{ijk} is the Levi-Civita symbol, which is defined as \epsilon_{123} = 1 and is antisymmetric in its indices (i.e. \epsilon_{ijk} = -\epsilon_{jik}, and \epsilon_{iij} = 0). As not all commutation relations are zero the algebra \mathfrak{su}(2) is non-abelian. The commutation relations for \mathfrak{su}(3) are more complicated, but they have a similar structure to those of \mathfrak{su}(2), built from generators T_a where a runs from 1 to 8.

title: 1: The Playground
2: Representations
text:
In the last post we looked at groups, algebras, and Casimir invariants. This time, we’re going to dive deep into the world of representation theory, and begin connecting the abstract mathematical structures of groups and algebras to the physical properties of particles in our universe.
A ‘representation’ of an algebra is a way of expressing the elements of the algebra as matrices, and the algebra operation as matrix multiplication. There are generally multiple different representations of a given algebra, and each representation can be thought of as a different way of realizing the symmetries of the algebra.
Let’s walk through the details of how to build representations using the example of the algebra \mathfrak{su}(2), which is part of the full algebra we are looking at. First, we need to explore the structure of \mathfrak{su}(2) a bit more, and introduce the Cartan-Weyl basis of the algebra.


We discussed last time that \mathfrak{su}(2) has three generators J_i and a single Casimir invariant \mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{su}(2)} = J_i J_i. The presence of a single Casimir invariant means that the representations of \mathfrak{su}(2) are classified by a single number, which we will call j. This number is related to the eigenvalue of the Casimir invariant in the representation. The single Casimir invariant also means that there is a single-element subalgebra H of \mathfrak{su}(2), called the Cartan subalgebra, which is an abelian subalgebra. For the sake of definiteness we will denote it by H=J_3. We can now carry out a mathematical trick, and make some more operators: E^+=J_1 + i J_2 and E^-=J_1 - i J_2. Plugging these definitions into the commutation relations for \mathfrak{su}(2), we find that [H, E^\pm] = \pm E^\pm and [E^+, E^-] = 2H. This is the Cartan-Weyl basis of the algebra: it consists of the Cartan subalgebra H, and the raising and lowering operators E^+ and E^-, whose names will make sense shortly.
We are going to assume that the representation of \mathfrak{su}(2) we are looking at is finite-dimensional, which means that the matrices that represent the generators are finite in size. Let’s assume that we have vector space V on which the generators of \mathfrak{su}(2) act. We can find a vector \ket{v} in this space which is an eigenvector of H, so H\ket{v}=v \ket{v} for some eigenvalue v. We call v the ‘weight’ of the vector \ket{v}. Now let’s act on \ket{v} with the raising operator E^+, and measure the weight of this new vector using H:

H(E^+\ket{v}) = (E^+H + [H, E^+])\ket{v} = (E^+v + E^+)\ket{v} = (v + 1) E^+\ket{v} = (v+1)\ket{v+1}.

So E^+ has created a new vector with weight v+1 (hence the name ‘raising operator’). Note that the weight has increased by exactly 1: it is not a continuous change, but discrete. We could keep applying E^+ to generate vectors with higher and higher weight; but we are assuming that the representation is finite-dimensional, so at some point we must reach a vector \ket{j} such that E^+ \ket{j} = 0. Let us call the eigenvalue of H on this vector j, so H\ket{j} = j \ket{j}. We can now apply the lowering operator E^- to generate vectors with lower and lower weight (again going down in steps of 1), until we reach a vector \ket{j_\mathrm{min}} such that E^- \ket{j_\mathrm{min}} = 0 (the series must terminate because we assume a finite-dimensional representation). In the box below we prove that j_\mathrm{min}=-j, and hence there are exactly 2j+1 vectors in the representation, with weights ranging from -j to j in integer steps.






NoteProof that j_\mathrm{min}=-j





We know two things about the vector \ket{j}: it has weight j, so H\ket{j} = j \ket{j}, and it is annihilated by the raising operator, so E^+ \ket{j} = 0. Let’s try applying the lowering operator E^- to \ket{j}, and then going back up with E^+: 
E^+ (E^- \ket{j}) = ([E^+, E^-] + E^- E^+)\ket{j} = (2H + 0)\ket{j} = 2j \ket{j}.

We can do something similar with an arbitrary vector \ket{m} which is the result of applying the lowering operator j-m times to \ket{j}, so \ket{m} = (E^-)^{j-m} \ket{j}: let us first define the eigenvalue of E^+E^- on this vector as E^+E^- \ket{m} = f(m) \ket{m}. Now let us consider the vector \ket{m-1} = E^- \ket{m}, and apply E^+E^- to it:

\begin{aligned}
E^+E^-\ket{m-1} &= f(m-1)\ket{m-1} = E^+E^- E^- \ket{m} \\
&= ( E^- E^+ + 2H) E^- \ket{m} \\
&= E^- f(m)\ket{m} + 2(m-1)E^- \ket{m} \\
&= (f(m) + 2(m-1)) \ket{m-1}.
\end{aligned}
 so 
f(m-1) = f(m) + 2(m-1).
 This is an arithmetic progression: with the starting point f(j)=2j we can solve it to find that f(m)=j(j+1)-m(m-1). Now we know that f(j_\mathrm{min})=0 (because E^- \ket{j_\mathrm{min}} = 0), so we can solve for j_\mathrm{min} to find that j_\mathrm{min}=-j. \blacksquare



Now to connect to some physics. The number j is called the ‘spin’ of the representation, and in order for 2j+1 to be an integer, j can take on any non-negative half-integer value (i.e. 0, \frac{1}{2}, 1, \frac{3}{2}, 2, \ldots). This is exactly the spin associated with all the particles of the Standard Model.



The Cartan-Weyl basis and the method of raising and lowering operators can be applied to any simple Lie algebra, including \mathfrak{su}(3). Because \mathfrak{su}(3) has two Casimir invariants, its representations are classified by two integers, which we can call p and q. The raising and lowering operators of \mathfrak{su}(3) come in pairs, and they raise and lower the weights in a two-dimensional weight space. The representations of \mathfrak{su}(3) are more complicated than those of \mathfrak{su}(2), but they can still be constructed using the same principles. The representation labels (p, q) are both integers, rather than half-integers, but this is just a normalization convention: the spin of \mathfrak{su}(2) is a half-integer because we have normalized the generators such that the raising and lowering operators change the weight by 1, whereas for \mathfrak{su}(3) we have normalized the generators such that the raising and lowering operators change the weights by 1 in a two-dimensional weight space. The representations of \mathfrak{su}(3) are important in particle physics, as they correspond to the different types of quarks and gluons in the Standard Model.
As an abelian algebra, \mathfrak{u}(1) has only one-dimensional representations, which are classified by a single integer (or half-integer) charge Y.
The representations of \mathfrak{so}(4,1) are more complicated. Because the algebra is non-compact, its representations are infinite-dimensional. We can split the two labels (as there are two Casimir invariants) into a continuous label \nu (which will end up related to mass) and a discrete label s (which will end up related to spin).

title: 2: Representations
text:
In the last post we looked at groups, algebras, and Casimir invariants. This time, we’re going to dive deep into the world of representation theory, and begin connecting the abstract mathematical structures of groups and algebras to the physical properties of particles in our universe.
A ‘representation’ of an algebra is a way of expressing the elements of the algebra as matrices, and the algebra operation as matrix multiplication. There are generally multiple different representations of a given algebra, and each representation can be thought of as a different way of realizing the symmetries of the algebra.
Let’s walk through the details of how to build representations using the example of the algebra \mathfrak{su}(2), which is part of the full algebra we are looking at. First, we need to explore the structure of \mathfrak{su}(2) a bit more, and introduce the Cartan-Weyl basis of the algebra.


We discussed last time that \mathfrak{su}(2) has three generators J_i and a single Casimir invariant \mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{su}(2)} = J_i J_i. The presence of a single Casimir invariant means that the representations of \mathfrak{su}(2) are classified by a single number, which we will call j. This number is related to the eigenvalue of the Casimir invariant in the representation. The single Casimir invariant also means that there is a single-element subalgebra H of \mathfrak{su}(2), called the Cartan subalgebra, which is an abelian subalgebra. Plugging these definitions into the commutation relations for \mathfrak{su}(2), we find that [H, E^\pm] = \pm E^\pm and [E^+, E^-] = 2H. This is the Cartan-Weyl basis of the algebra: it consists of the Cartan subalgebra H, and the raising and lowering operators E^+ and E^-, whose names will make sense shortly.
We are going to assume that the representation of \mathfrak{su}(2) we are looking at is finite-dimensional, which means that the matrices that represent the generators are finite in size. We can find a vector \ket{v} in this space which is an eigenvector of H, so H\ket{v}=v \ket{v} for some eigenvalue v. We call v the ‘weight’ of the vector \ket{v}. Now let’s act on \ket{v} with the raising operator E^+, and measure the weight of this new vector using H:

Let us call the eigenvalue of H on this vector j, so H\ket{j} = j \ket{j}. We can now apply the lowering operator E^- to generate vectors with lower and lower weight (again going down in steps of 1), until we reach a vector \ket{j_\mathrm{min}} such that E^- \ket{j_\mathrm{min}} = 0 (the series must terminate because we assume a finite-dimensional representation). In the box below we prove that j_\mathrm{min}=-j, and hence there are exactly 2j+1 vectors in the representation, with weights ranging from -j to j in integer steps.






NoteProof that j_\mathrm{min}=-j





We know two things about the vector \ket{j}: it has weight j, so H\ket{j} = j \ket{j}, and it is annihilated by the raising operator, so E^+ \ket{j} = 0. Let’s try applying the lowering operator E^- to \ket{j}, and then going back up with E^+: 
E^+ (E^- \ket{j}) = ([E^+, E^-] + E^- E^+)\ket{j} = (2H + 0)\ket{j} = 2j \ket{j}.

We can do something similar with an arbitrary vector \ket{m} which is the result of applying the lowering operator j-m times to \ket{j}, so \ket{m} = (E^-)^{j-m} \ket{j}: let us first define the eigenvalue of E^+E^- on this vector as E^+E^- \ket{m} = f(m) \ket{m}. Now let us consider the vector \ket{m-1} = E^- \ket{m}, and apply E^+E^- to it:

\begin{aligned}
E^+E^-\ket{m-1} &= f(m-1)\ket{m-1} = E^+E^- E^- \ket{m} \\
&= ( E^- E^+ + 2H) E^- \ket{m} \\
&= E^- f(m)\ket{m} + 2(m-1)E^- \ket{m} \\
&= (f(m) + 2(m-1)) \ket{m-1}.
\end{aligned}
 so 
f(m-1) = f(m) + 2(m-1).
 This is an arithmetic progression: with the starting point f(j)=2j we can solve it to find that f(m)=j(j+1)-m(m-1). Now we know that f(j_\mathrm{min})=0 (because E^- \ket{j_\mathrm{min}} = 0), so we can solve for j_\mathrm{min} to find that j_\mathrm{min}=-j. \blacksquare



Now to connect to some physics. The number j is called the ‘spin’ of the representation, and in order for 2j+1 to be an integer, j can take on any non-negative half-integer value (i.e. 0, \frac{1}{2}, 1, \frac{3}{2}, 2, \ldots). This is exactly the spin associated with all the particles of the Standard Model.



The Cartan-Weyl basis and the method of raising and lowering operators can be applied to any simple Lie algebra, including \mathfrak{su}(3). Because \mathfrak{su}(3) has two Casimir invariants, its representations are classified by two integers, which we can call p and q. The raising and lowering operators of \mathfrak{su}(3) come in pairs, and they raise and lower the weights in a two-dimensional weight space. The representations of \mathfrak{su}(3) are more complicated than those of \mathfrak{su}(2), but they can still be constructed using the same principles. The representation labels (p, q) are both integers, rather than half-integers, but this is just a normalization convention: the spin of \mathfrak{su}(2) is a half-integer because we have normalized the generators such that the raising and lowering operators change the weight by 1, whereas for \mathfrak{su}(3) we have normalized the generators such that the raising and lowering operators change the weights by 1 in a two-dimensional weight space. The representations of \mathfrak{su}(3) are important in particle physics, as they correspond to the different types of quarks and gluons in the Standard Model.
As an abelian algebra, \mathfrak{u}(1) has only one-dimensional representations, which are classified by a single integer (or half-integer) charge Y.
The representations of \mathfrak{so}(4,1) are more complicated. Because the algebra is non-compact, its representations are infinite-dimensional. We can split the two labels (as there are two Casimir invariants) into a continuous label \nu (which will end up related to mass) and a discrete label s (which will end up related to spin).

title: 2: Representations
text:
2 Casimir values
We have seen that the representations of the algebras can be labelled by a set of numbers (e.g. j for \mathfrak{su}(2), and (p, q) for \mathfrak{su}(3)). Physically it can be more intuitive to label the representations by the values of the Casimir invariants, as these are directly related to measurable physical properties of particles and fields. We conclude this post by summarising the values of the Casimir invariants in the different representations.
For \mathfrak{su}(2) the value of the Casimir invariant \mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{su}(2)} in the representation with spin j is given by \mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{su}(2)} = j(j+1), familiar from angular momentum in quantum mechanics. For \mathfrak{u}(1) the value of the Casimir invariant \mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{u}(1)} in the representation with charge Y is given by \mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{u}(1)} = Y^2. For \mathfrak{su}(3) the value of the Casimir invariant \mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{su}(3)} in the representation with labels (p, q) is given by \mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{su}(3)} =  rac{1}{3} (p^2 + q^2 + pq + 3p + 3q) and \mathcal{C}_3^{\mathfrak{su}(3)}= rac{1}{18} (p-q)(2p+q+3)(p+2q+3).
For \mathfrak{so}(4,1) the values of the Casimir invariants in the representation with labels (
u, s) are given by \mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{so}(4,1)} = -
u^2 - s(s+1) -  rac{9}{4} and \mathcal{C}_4^{\mathfrak{so}(4,1)} = -s(s+1)(
u^2 +  rac{1}{4}). There are three different branches of representations of \mathfrak{so}(4,1): a primary branch, where 
u is a real number; a complementary branch where 
u is pure imaginary; and a discrete series, where 
u=s+1 takes on specific values. In the next post we will connect these values to physical properties of particles in our constructed universe.

title: 4: Fields on the Group
text:
So far we have discussed symmetries of our theory, and the mathematical structures that underly them. But we haven’t yet had any dynamical, physical objects that can act under those symmetries. This post will introduce the concept of fields on our group, and we will see how the symmetries of the group act on those fields and strongly constrain their form.
Recall that our full group is G=\mathrm{SO}(4,1)\times \mathrm{U}(1) \times \mathrm{SU}(2) \times \mathrm{SU}(3). A ‘group field’ is a complex-valued function defined on r copies of this group, \phi(g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_r), where each g_i \in G is a group element. This is the central dynamical object of our theory. Let’s now dive into what this field means, and how it connects to the symmetries of our group.

title: 4: Fields on the Group
text:
So far we have discussed symmetries of our theory, and the mathematical structures that underly them. But we haven’t yet had any dynamical, physical objects that can act under those symmetries. This post will introduce the concept of fields on our group, and we will see how the symmetries of the group act on those fields and strongly constrain their form.
Recall that our full group is G=\mathrm{SO}(4,1)\times \mathrm{U}(1) \times \mathrm{SU}(2) \times \mathrm{SU}(3). A ‘group field’ is a complex-valued function defined on r copies of this group, \phi(g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_r), where each g_i \in G is a group element. This is the central dynamical object of our theory. Let’s now dive into what this field means, and how it connects to the symmetries of our group.

title: 4: Fields on the Group
text:
2 What is a field?
At this point the field \phi(g_1, \ldots, g_r) is a purely group-theoretic object: it lives on r copies of the group manifold G, and we have no connection to any physical theory. The question of what r should be, and what geometric picture (if any) emerges, will be answered from the group structure alone. We will now impose two natural constraints on the field, gauge invariance and simplicity, and show that together they force r to take a specific value and endow the field with a geometric interpretation.

2.1 Gauge invariance and closure
The first constraint comes from gauge symmetry. We want that the physics of our universe does not change if we ‘rotate’ our viewpoint of the fields using any of the symmetries of the group. This can be written by requiring that the field is invariant under a group element acting simultaneously on every argument of the field by right multiplication:

\phi(g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_r) = \phi(g_1 h, g_2 h, \ldots, g_r h), \qquad \forall\, h \in G.
\tag{1}
This is the statement that the physics does not depend on a simultaneous ‘rotation’ of all arguments by the same group element. At the level of the Lie algebra, this gauge invariance translates into a ‘closure constraint’: the Lie algebra elements (or ‘fluxes’) associated with each argument must sum to zero,

\sum_{i=1}^r X_i = 0, \qquad X_i \in \mathfrak{g}.
\tag{2}
Think of this like conservation of angular momentum: the total flux across all arguments must balance. Since the algebra decomposes as a direct sum (see the first post), this closure constraint holds independently for each factor. The constraint does not yet tell us what r is, but as we will see, when combined with a second constraint it will give r a powerful geometric interpretation.


2.2 The maximal compact subgroup and \mathfrak{so}(4)
To go further, we need to look more carefully at the structure of \mathrm{SO}(4,1). This group is non-compact (because of the -1 in the metric signature), and its representation theory is correspondingly complicated. However, every non-compact semisimple Lie group has a ‘maximal compact subgroup’, which captures much of the representation-theoretic structure while being far easier to work with.
For \mathrm{SO}(4,1), the maximal compact subgroup is \mathrm{SO}(4): the group of rotations in four-dimensional Euclidean space, corresponding to the generators M_{AB} with A, B \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\} (i.e. the spatial indices only, excluding the timelike 0 direction). It is this subgroup that the simplicity constraint will act on.
The algebra \mathfrak{so}(4) has a beautiful structure. Its generators are the six antisymmetric matrices M_{ab} with a, b \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}, which we can organise into two sets of three by defining the self-dual and anti-self-dual combinations:

\begin{aligned}
J_i^+ &= \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}\epsilon_{ijk} M_{jk} + M_{i4}\right), \\
J_i^- &= \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}\epsilon_{ijk} M_{jk} - M_{i4}\right),
\end{aligned}
\tag{3}
where i, j, k \in \{1, 2, 3\}. One can verify using the commutation relations above (restricted to spatial indices with \eta_{ab}=\delta_{ab}) that these satisfy

[J_i^+, J_j^+] = \epsilon_{ijk} J_k^+, \qquad [J_i^-, J_j^-] = \epsilon_{ijk} J_k^-, \qquad [J_i^+, J_j^-] = 0.
\tag{4}
The two sets of generators decouple completely! This means the algebra splits as a direct sum:

\mathfrak{so}(4) \cong \mathfrak{su}(2)^+ \oplus \mathfrak{su}(2)^-.
\tag{5}
Therefore the representations of \mathfrak{so}(4) are labelled by a pair (j^+, j^-) of \mathfrak{su}(2) spins. This is a key result: the representation theory of \mathrm{SO}(4) is fully captured by two independent \mathfrak{su}(2) spin labels.


2.3 The simplicity constraint
Each argument g_i of the field lives in \mathrm{SO}(4,1) (times the internal groups). At the Lie algebra level, the data associated with each argument is an element B = \frac{1}{2} B^{AB} M_{AB} \in \mathfrak{so}(4,1) (compare to the way a vector is defined on a basis). The generators M_{AB} are antisymmetric in their indices (M_{AB} = -M_{BA}), so B is characterised by the 10 independent components B^{AB}. Because B is defined analogously to a vector on a vector space, but is a rank-2 object, it is called a ‘bivector’.
Now, a general bivector is a sum of independent ‘wedge products’: it can encode data from multiple independent planes simultaneously. In \mathfrak{so}(4,1), which has a 5-dimensional defining representation, a generic bivector can be decomposed into at most \lfloor 5/2 \rfloor = 2 independent simple (wedge-product) pieces. The ‘simple’ bivectors are those that consist of a single wedge product: B=u\wedge v \implies B^{AB} = u^A v^B - u^B v^A for some vectors u^A, v^A.

There is a clean algebraic condition for simplicity. A bivector B^{AB} is simple if and only if

\epsilon^{ABCDE} B_{AB} B_{CD} = 0.
\tag{6}
The left-hand side is formed by contracting B \wedge B with the totally antisymmetric Levi-Civita symbol. This expression vanishes if and only if B is simple (i.e. B = u \wedge v). When B is instead a sum of two or more independent wedge products, the contraction is non-zero. This condition is purely algebraic and it is called the ‘simplicity constraint’.


2.4 Simplicity at the level of representations
Now let’s translate this condition into representation theory, using the \mathfrak{so}(4) \cong \mathfrak{su}(2)^+ \oplus \mathfrak{su}(2)^- decomposition from Equation 5, and apply it to our field. An arbitrary element B_{ab} of \mathfrak{so}(4) decomposes into self-dual and anti-self-dual parts via Equation 3:

B_i^+ = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}\epsilon_{ijk} B_{jk} + B_{i4}\right), \qquad B_i^- = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}\epsilon_{ijk} B_{jk} - B_{i4}\right).

The simplicity condition Equation 6, when restricted to the \mathrm{SO}(4) sector (indices 1–4 only), becomes

|B^+|^2 = |B^-|^2,
\tag{7}
where |B^\pm|^2 = B_i^\pm B_i^\pm are the norms of the self-dual and anti-self-dual parts respectively.






NoteDerivation of Equation 7 from Equation 6





In four dimensions, the simplicity condition \epsilon^{abcd} B_{ab} B_{cd} = 0 can be evaluated by expanding the Levi-Civita symbol. The result is easiest in the self-dual/anti-self-dual basis. Using the decomposition B_{jk} = \epsilon_{jki}(B_i^+ + B_i^-) and B_{i4} = B_i^+ - B_i^-, the simplicity condition becomes

\epsilon^{abcd} B_{ab} B_{cd} \propto |B^+|^2 - |B^-|^2 = 0.\blacksquare




Recall from Equation 5 that \mathfrak{so}(4) representations are labelled (j^+, j^-). The Casimir invariant of each \mathfrak{su}(2)^\pm factor is J_i^\pm J_i^\pm, with eigenvalue j^\pm(j^\pm+1) in the representation labelled by j^\pm.
In the next post we will take the Peter-Weyl transform of the field, analogous to a Fourier transform on group space. To see what the simplicity constraint becomes in this picture, consider the analogy with ordinary Fourier analysis. A function f(x) on a line can be expanded in modes e^{ikx}. Classically, momentum is a number p; but in the Fourier picture, the role of momentum is played by the differential operator -i\frac{d}{dx}, whose eigenvalues are the Fourier labels k. The classical quantity p is replaced by k — a new discrete (or continuous) parameter that labels the modes.
The same structure applies to functions on a group. Our field \phi(g_1, g_2, \ldots, g_r) is a function on the group, and the Peter-Weyl theorem expands it in irreducible representation matrix elements (the group-theoretic analogue of e^{ikx}). The Lie algebra flux B_i^\pm, which classically is just a set of numerical components, plays the role of momentum: it is replaced by the generator J_i^\pm, which acts on functions on the group by differentiation (a left-invariant vector field), and whose Casimir eigenvalue j^\pm(j^\pm+1) is the new label that characterises each mode. So the correspondence is: B_i^\pm (classical flux) \;\longleftrightarrow\; j^\pm (representation label), just as p (classical momentum) \;\longleftrightarrow\; k (Fourier label). The classical simplicity condition |B^+|^2 = |B^-|^2 therefore becomes a condition on these new labels: j^+(j^+ + 1) = j^-(j^- + 1), and since spins are non-negative, this gives

j^+ = j^-.
\tag{8}
This is the simplicity constraint in representation-theoretic language: it restricts the \mathrm{SO}(4) representations that appear in our field to the diagonal ones, where the self-dual and anti-self-dual spins are equal.
This is a powerful result. It means that each argument of our field, when we decompose its \mathrm{SO}(4,1) content into \mathrm{SO}(4) representations, must carry equal left and right spins.

title: 4: Fields on the Group
text:
3 From simple bivectors to triangles
Before we can determine r, we need to understand what each argument of the field represents after simplicity has been imposed.
The maximal compact subgroup of \mathrm{SO}(4,1) is \mathrm{SO}(4), which acts naturally on \mathbb{R}^4. A simple bivector in \mathfrak{so}(4) is an element B_{ab} = u_a v_b - u_b v_a for two vectors u, v \in \mathbb{R}^4. Geometrically, this is the oriented area element of the parallelogram spanned by u and v: or equivalently, of a triangle with two edges along u and v. So the simplicity constraint has told us, purely from the algebra, that each argument of the field carries the data of a triangular face embedded in \mathbb{R}^4.

title: 4: Fields on the Group
text:
4 Why r = 4
We can now combine the closure constraint (Section 2.1) with the geometric content of simplicity (Section 3). The closure constraint on the \mathfrak{so}(4) sector requires

\sum_{i=1}^r B^{(i)}_{ab} = 0,
\tag{9}
where each B^{(i)}_{ab} is a simple bivector representing a triangular face. This algebraic condition on the bivectors is a consequence of the gauge invariance Equation 1 that we already imposed. With the interpretation of B^{(i)}_{ab} as the oriented area of a triangle, the closure condition implies that the oriented area data associated with the r arguments of the field must form a closed surface; open configurations are not gauge-invariant and not allowed.
The mathematical content of this closure is captured by the ‘generalised Minkowski theorem’: a set of r simple bivectors in \mathbb{R}^n that sum to zero correspond one-to-one with the oriented face areas of a closed convex polyhedron. In our case n=4, as determined by \mathrm{SO}(4) \subset \mathrm{SO}(4,1).
So which closed polyhedron? The field \phi has a fixed number r of arguments, and our task is to find the minimal value of r for which closure is possible. We want the minimal value of r because we want the field to be as simple as possible. If we constructed a non-minimal polyhedron with more faces, we could always triangulate it into a collection of smaller polyhedra with fewer faces, and therefore build it from products of fields with fewer arguments. The minimal value of r gives us the fundamental building block of geometry in our theory.
A closed surface made of triangles requires at least 4 triangular faces. The unique closed triangulated surface with exactly 4 faces is the boundary of a tetrahedron.
Notice the logical chain: we chose \mathrm{SO}(4,1) as the symmetry group \to its maximal compact subgroup is \mathrm{SO}(4) \to simple bivectors in \mathfrak{so}(4) describe triangles in \mathbb{R}^4 \to the minimal set of triangles satisfying the closure constraint is a tetrahedron with 4 faces \to

r = 4.
\tag{10}
Our field therefore takes the form

\phi(g_1, g_2, g_3, g_4), \qquad g_i \in G,
\tag{11}
subject to the gauge invariance Equation 1 and the simplicity constraint Equation 8. Each such field describes the state of a single tetrahedron, equipped with internal gauge charges from the \mathrm{U}(1) \times \mathrm{SU}(2) \times \mathrm{SU}(3) factors.
Let’s summarise the process we have carried out in this post. We did not (and will not for some time) use any spacetime manifold or dimension. We defined a field on copies of an abstract group, imposed natural constraints (gauge invariance and simplicity), and the group told us the answer: because \mathrm{SO}(4,1) has \mathrm{SO}(4) as its maximal compact subgroup, the geometry that emerges is four-dimensional, and the fundamental building block is a tetrahedron. Had we started with \mathrm{SO}(3,1) (with maximal compact \mathrm{SO}(3)), simple bivectors in \mathfrak{so}(3) would be vectors in \mathbb{R}^3; the closure constraint would give a closed polygon of edges; the simplest such polygon would be a triangle with r=3, and we would be building three-dimensional geometry.
Later in this series we will see how the dynamics of the theory describe how these tetrahedra glue together, and how spacetime emerges from their collective behaviour. But for now, we have a beautiful geometric picture of the fundamental ‘atom of geometry’ in our theory: the group field \phi(g_1, g_2, g_3, g_4) describes a single tetrahedron, with its four arguments encoding the geometry of its four triangular faces.
In the next post we will come back to the Peter-Weyl transform mentioned above and bring our Casimir operators back to the fore, beginning to form the foundations of the dynamics of our theory.

title: About
text: This blog is written by Tom Whitehead. I am the Head of Machine Learning at Intellegens, where we develop and deploy a machine learning toolkit for experimental design in the materials, chemicals, formulations, and life sciences industry. I have a background in physics and machine learning, and enjoy solving and writing about complex problems in these fields.

title: Beginner’s Guide to Group Field Theory
text:
Welcome to this set of posts discussing group field theory! This is a topic in theoretical physics that aims to build a picture of the universe starting from fundamental symmetries and interactions. The idea is to use mathematical structures called groups to describe the symmetries of physical systems, and then to build up to a realistic picture of our universe from there.
This is a beginner’s guide, both through aiming to be accessible to those without a full background in the topic, and through this being a relatively new area for me! I am keen to extend my understanding of the topic, and to share that understanding with others.

title: 5: The Peter-Weyl Transform
text: Last time we introduced the concept of fields on our group, and we saw how the symmetries of the group act on those fields. We will now do the equivalent of ‘going to momentum space’ in our theory, by performing a Peter-Weyl transform on the field.

title: 5: The Peter-Weyl Transform
text: Last time we introduced the concept of fields on our group, and we saw how the symmetries of the group act on those fields. We will now do the equivalent of ‘going to momentum space’ in our theory, by performing a Peter-Weyl transform on the field.

title: 5: The Peter-Weyl Transform
section: 2 The Peter-Weyl transform and the Haar measure
text:
2 The Peter-Weyl transform and the Haar measure
Now that we know the field lives on G^4, we would like to decompose it into modes:the analogue of a Fourier transform. On a Lie group, the correct tool is the Peter-Weyl theorem, which generalises the Fourier transform from the circle group \mathrm{U}(1) to an arbitrary compact group.
For a compact group K, the Peter-Weyl theorem states that the matrix elements of irreducible representations form a complete orthonormal basis for square-integrable functions on K with respect to the Haar measure \mathrm{d}\mu(k). The Haar measure is the unique (up to normalisation) left- and right-invariant measure on the group: it is the natural ‘volume element’ that respects the group’s symmetries.
For our compact factors this is straightforward. On \mathrm{SU}(2), the Haar measure can be parametrised using the Euler angles (\alpha, \beta, \gamma) as

\mathrm{d}\mu_{\mathrm{SU}(2)} = \frac{1}{16\pi^2} \sin\beta\, \mathrm{d}\alpha\, \mathrm{d}\beta\, \mathrm{d}\gamma,

with ranges \alpha \in [0, 2\pi), \beta \in [0, \pi], \gamma \in [0, 4\pi). For \mathrm{SU}(3), the Haar measure involves eight parameters (corresponding to the eight generators) and is considerably more involved, but has a similar essential character. For \mathrm{U}(1), the Haar measure is simply \mathrm{d}\theta/(2\pi).
For the non-compact factor \mathrm{SO}(4,1), the situation is more subtle. We need to use a generalization of the Peter-Weyl theorem known as the Plancherel decomposition which handles non-compact groups. The sum over representations becomes an integral, weighted by the Plancherel measure \mu(\nu, s), which depends on the representation labels (\nu, s) introduced in the representations post.
To write the Haar measure on \mathrm{SO}(4,1) explicitly, we use the Cartan decomposition \mathrm{SO}(4,1) = KAK, where K = \mathrm{SO}(4) is the maximal compact subgroup discussed in the last post and A is a one-dimensional abelian subgroup parametrised by a ‘boost’ parameter t \geq 0. This decomposition says that any element of \mathrm{SO}(4,1) can be written as a rotation, followed by a boost, followed by another rotation. The Haar measure then factorises as

\mathrm{d}\mu_{\mathrm{SO}(4,1)} = \mathrm{d}\mu_{\mathrm{SO}(4)} \cdot \sinh^3(t)\, \mathrm{d}t \cdot \mathrm{d}\mu_{\mathrm{SO}(4)}.

We can make this completely explicit using the decomposition \mathrm{SO}(4) \cong (\mathrm{SU}(2)^+ \times \mathrm{SU}(2)^-)/\mathbb{Z}_2 from the self-dual/anti-self-dual splitting discussed last time. (Recall that the algebra splits as a clean direct sum \mathfrak{su}(2)^+ \oplus \mathfrak{su}(2)^-; the \mathbb{Z}_2 quotient only appears at the group level because the element (-I,-I) \in \mathrm{SU}(2)^+ \times \mathrm{SU}(2)^- acts trivially on \mathbb{R}^4, making the product a double cover of \mathrm{SO}(4). For the Haar measure this is invisible: the quotient is discrete, so it only halves the total volume, which is absorbed into the normalisation.) Each \mathrm{SO}(4) Haar measure factorises into two \mathrm{SU}(2) Euler-angle measures,

\mathrm{d}\mu_{\mathrm{SO}(4)} = \frac{1}{(16\pi^2)^2}\, \sin\beta^+\, \mathrm{d}\alpha^+\, \mathrm{d}\beta^+\, \mathrm{d}\gamma^+\;\sin\beta^-\, \mathrm{d}\alpha^-\, \mathrm{d}\beta^-\, \mathrm{d}\gamma^-,

with the same ranges as the \mathrm{SU}(2) measure above: \alpha^\pm \in [0,2\pi), \beta^\pm \in [0,\pi], \gamma^\pm \in [0,4\pi). Writing subscripts 1,2 for the two \mathrm{SO}(4) copies and expanding everything out, the full Haar measure on \mathrm{SO}(4,1) reads

\mathrm{d}\mu_{\mathrm{SO}(4,1)} = \frac{1}{(16\pi^2)^4}\;\sinh^3(t)\;\prod_{a=1}^{2}\prod_{\sigma=\pm} \sin\beta_a^{\sigma}\;\mathrm{d}\alpha_a^{\sigma}\,\mathrm{d}\beta_a^{\sigma}\,\mathrm{d}\gamma_a^{\sigma}\;\mathrm{d}t,

with thirteen integration variables (6+1+6): twelve Euler angles and one boost parameter t \geq 0. Note that \mathrm{SO}(4,1) is only ten-dimensional; the three extra variables reflect the redundancy of the KAK decomposition: the centraliser M = Z_K(\mathfrak{a}) \cong \mathrm{SO}(3) (dim 3) acts by k_1 a_t k_2 = (k_1 m)\, a_t\,(m^{-1} k_2), so different triples (k_1, t, k_2) can describe the same group element. This overcounting is absorbed into the normalisation constant.
The \sinh^3(t) factor is characteristic of the de Sitter geometry: it reflects the fact that the volume of a sphere in hyperbolic space grows exponentially with radius. In the flat limit t \to 0 (corresponding to \ell \to \infty), \sinh^3(t) \approx t^3, recovering the familiar Euclidean volume element.
The full Haar measure on G^4 = [\mathrm{SO}(4,1)\times \mathrm{U}(1) \times \mathrm{SU}(2) \times \mathrm{SU}(3)]^4 is the product of four copies of each factor’s measure.

2.1 The mode expansion
Why did we need the Haar measure? The Peter-Weyl theorem says that the matrix elements of the irreducible representations form a complete orthonormal basis for L^2(K, \mathrm{d}\mu): the space of square-integrable functions on the group with respect to the Haar measure. The measure defines the inner product, giving meaning to the phrase ‘orthonormal basis’. In other words, the Haar measure is to L^2(K) what \mathrm{d}^3 x is to L^2(\mathbb{R}^3): the weight that makes the Fourier-type expansion work.
The Peter-Weyl (or Plancherel) expansion of our field then reads:

\phi(g_1, g_2, g_3, g_4) = \sum_{\{J_i\}} \phi^{J_1 J_2 J_3 J_4}_{m_1 m_2 m_3 m_4, n_1 n_2 n_3 n_4} \prod_{i=1}^4 D^{J_i}_{m_i n_i}(g_i),
\tag{1}
where J_i collectively denotes all the representation labels for each copy of G (i.e. (\nu_i, s_i) for \mathrm{SO}(4,1), Y_i for \mathrm{U}(1), j_i for \mathrm{SU}(2), and (p_i, q_i) for \mathrm{SU}(3)), and D^{J_i}_{m_i n_i}(g_i) are the corresponding matrix elements of the irreducible representations (Wigner D-matrices and their generalisations). For the \mathrm{SO}(4,1) factor, the sum over \nu is replaced by an integral weighted by the Plancherel measure. The simplicity constraint restricts the \mathrm{SO}(4) content of the \mathrm{SO}(4,1) representations to the diagonal j^+ = j^- sector.


2.2 Gauge invariance in representation space
The gauge invariance has a striking effect when translated into this representation basis. Recall that gauge invariance requires \phi(g_1 h, g_2 h, g_3 h, g_4 h) = \phi(g_1, g_2, g_3, g_4) for all h \in G. Under right multiplication by h, the matrix elements transform as

D^{J}_{mn}(g h) = \sum_{k} D^{J}_{mk}(g)\, D^{J}_{kn}(h).

Substituting the expansion Equation 1 into the gauge invariance condition and using the orthogonality of the representation matrices, we find that the coefficients \phi^{J_1 J_2 J_3 J_4}_{m_1 m_2 m_3 m_4, n_1 n_2 n_3 n_4} must satisfy

\sum_{k_1 k_2 k_3 k_4} \phi^{J_1 J_2 J_3 J_4}_{m_1 m_2 m_3 m_4, k_1 k_2 k_3 k_4} \, D^{J_1}_{k_1 n_1}(h)\, D^{J_2}_{k_2 n_2}(h)\, D^{J_3}_{k_3 n_3}(h)\, D^{J_4}_{k_4 n_4}(h) = \phi^{J_1 J_2 J_3 J_4}_{m_1 m_2 m_3 m_4, n_1 n_2 n_3 n_4}

for all h. By Schur’s lemma, this is only possible if the n_i indices are contracted with an ‘intertwiner’: a tensor \iota^{J_1 J_2 J_3 J_4}_{n_1 n_2 n_3 n_4} that is invariant under the simultaneous action of G on all four representation spaces:

\sum_{n_1 n_2 n_3 n_4} \iota^{J_1 J_2 J_3 J_4}_{n_1 n_2 n_3 n_4}\, D^{J_1}_{n_1 n_1'}(h)\, D^{J_2}_{n_2 n_2'}(h)\, D^{J_3}_{n_3 n_3'}(h)\, D^{J_4}_{n_4 n_4'}(h) = \iota^{J_1 J_2 J_3 J_4}_{n_1' n_2' n_3' n_4'}, \quad \forall\, h \in G.
\tag{2}
Such intertwiners are built from Clebsch-Gordan coefficients: the tensors that couple pairs of representations into a third. For example, for the \mathrm{SU}(2) part of the group, one first couples j_1 \otimes j_2 into an intermediate representation k, and then j_3 \otimes j_4 into the same k, and contracts. The space of such intertwiners for four representations is generally multi-dimensional—labelled by the intermediate coupling k—which is why the intertwiner carries additional data beyond the four representation labels.
The gauge-invariant field therefore takes the form

\phi(g_1, g_2, g_3, g_4) = \sum_{\{J_i\}, \iota} \phi^{J_1 J_2 J_3 J_4}_{\iota,\, m_1 m_2 m_3 m_4} \;\iota^{J_1 J_2 J_3 J_4}_{n_1 n_2 n_3 n_4} \prod_{i=1}^4 D^{J_i}_{m_i n_i}(g_i),
\tag{3}
where the sum over \iota runs over a basis of the intertwiner space for the given set of representations \{J_i\}. The coefficients \phi^{J_1 J_2 J_3 J_4}_{\iota,\, m_1 m_2 m_3 m_4} are the remaining dynamical degrees of freedom. In the spin foam literature, the intertwiner \iota is often called a ‘spin network vertex’: it encodes the quantum state of the tetrahedron’s ‘shape’ (how the four face areas are coupled together), while the representation labels J_i encode the areas of the individual faces.
There we have it: a field defined on our group, including the constraints imposed by physical requirements, written in terms of the representation data that will allow us to connect to particles and fields in our emergent universe. Next time we will see how Casimir invariants act on these fields, and begin to move towards identifying aspects of the emergent physics.

title: 3: The Lorentz Connection
text:
This post will begin the journey from pure group theory towards a connection with the universe we are creating. The name ‘Lorentz connection’ is a bit of a pun, as we will be looking at how \mathfrak{so}(4,1) connects to the Lorentz algebra of symmetries in special relativity, but we won’t actually be building any spin connections or other explicit geometric structures in this post.
To start with, let’s remind ourselves of the algebra \mathfrak{so}(4,1). This was given in the first post as the algebra with generators M_{AB}, where A and B run from 0 to 4, and commutation relations

\left[M_{AB}, M_{CD}\right] = \eta_{AC} M_{BD} - \eta_{AD} M_{BC} - \eta_{BC} M_{AD} + \eta_{BD} M_{AC},
\tag{1}
where \eta_{AB}=\mathrm{diag}(-1,1,1,1,1). We will now connect this algebra back to the familiar maths of special relativity.
To start with, we will do a simple relabelling of the generators. Let’s define indices \mu and \nu that run from 0 to 3, and define J_{\mu\nu}=M_{\mu\nu} and P_\mu = M_{\mu 4}/\ell, where \ell is a constant with dimensions of length. We can now rewrite the commutation relations in terms of these new generators:

\begin{aligned}
\left[J_{\mu\nu}, J_{\rho\sigma}\right] &= \eta_{\mu\rho} J_{\nu\sigma} - \eta_{\mu\sigma} J_{\nu\rho} - \eta_{\nu\rho} J_{\mu\sigma} + \eta_{\nu\sigma} J_{\mu \rho}, \\
\left[J_{\mu\nu}, P_\rho\right] &= \eta_{\mu\rho} P_\nu - \eta_{\nu\rho} P_\mu, \\
\left[P_\mu, P_\nu\right] &= \frac{1}{\ell^2} J_{\mu\nu}.
\end{aligned}

Remember this is just a relabelling, none of the symmetries of the group have changed. But now we have some suggestive-shaped algebra: the first line is the algebra of Lorentz transformations, \mathfrak{so}(3,1), and the second line says that the P_\mu transform as a four-vector under Lorentz transformations. This line is also familiar from the Poincaré algebra of special relativity. The third line, however, is different from special relativity: it says that two translations do not commute, but instead give a rotation. This is a key feature of the algebra \mathfrak{so}(4,1), and it will have important consequences for the physics that emerges from this algebra. We write the separation of the algebra into these two parts as \mathfrak{so}(4,1) = \mathfrak{so}(3,1) + \mathfrak{p}, where \mathfrak{p} is the part of the algebra generated by the P_\mu. The group corresponding to the algebra \mathfrak{p} is called the de Sitter group, and is represented as the quotient group \mathrm{dS}_4=\mathrm{SO}(4,1)/\mathrm{SO}(3,1).
We can think of the non-commuting of translations like the behaviour of translations on a sphere: if you start at the north pole and translate down to the equator, then translate along the equator, and then translate back up to the north pole, you will be facing in a different direction than you started. This is a consequence of the curvature of the sphere, and it implies that the universe we are building also has a built-in curvature.
If we take the limit \ell \to \infty, the commutation relations reduce to those of the Poincaré algebra, which is the algebra of symmetries of flat spacetime in special relativity. This means that the algebra \mathfrak{so}(4,1) can be thought of as a ‘deformation’ of the Poincaré algebra, where the deformation parameter is \ell.

text:

This means that the algebra \mathfrak{so}(4,1) can be thought of as a ‘deformation’ of the Poincaré algebra, where the deformation parameter is \ell.

title: 3: The Lorentz Connection
section: 2 Casimir invariants in our new basis
text:
2 Casimir invariants in our new basis
The Casimir invariants of \mathfrak{so}(4,1) can also be rewritten in terms of the new generators. Recall that there is a second order and a fourth order Casimir invariant. Replacing the A, B indices with \mu, 
u and the 4 index with P_\mu gives us

\mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{so}(4,1)} =  rac{1}{2} \eta^{\muho} \eta^{
u\sigma} J_{\mu
u} J_{ho\sigma} + \ell^2 \eta^{\mu
u} P_\mu P_
u=\mathcal{C}_2^\mathrm{Lorentz} + \ell^2 P^2,

where \mathcal{C}_2^\mathrm{Lorentz} is the Casimir invariant of the Lorentz algebra \mathfrak{so}(3,1), and P^2 = \eta^{\mu
u} P_\mu P_
u. The fourth order Casimir invariant is given by

\mathcal{C}_4^\mathfrak{so}(4,1) = \eta_{\mu 
u} W^\mu W^
u + \left(	ilde{\mathcal{C}}_2^\mathrm{Lorentz}ight)^2,

where 	ilde{\mathcal{C}}_2^\mathrm{Lorentz}= rac{1}{8} \epsilon^{\mu
uho\sigma} J_{\mu
u} J_{ho\sigma} is the dual of the second order Casimir invariant of the Lorentz algebra, and W^\mu =  rac{1}{8} \epsilon^{\mu
uho\sigma} J_{
uho} P_\sigma is the Pauli-Lubanski vector from special relativity. So although our \mathfrak{so}(4,1) algebra is definitely 5-dimensional, it contains the familiar structures of special relativity within it. In the next post we will introduce the concept of fields on our group, and we will begin to connect our Casimir invariants and representations to those fields to construct the beginnings of a physcial theory.
